name: ginkgo biloba
mechanism: Proposed actions include platelet-activating factor antagonism and vascular/neurochemical effects from terpene lactones and flavonoids. These mechanistic hypotheses do not confirm clinical efficacy for major indications.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
safetyNotes: For most adults, ginkgo leaf extract appears reasonably safe in moderate oral amounts. It may increase bleeding risk (especially with anticoagulants) and may be unsafe during pregnancy.
description: Ginkgo biloba leaf extracts are marketed for memory and circulation-related goals, but clinical findings are inconsistent across populations and outcomes.
dosage: Dose depends on extract standardization and indication; trial doses are not interchangeable across products.

interactions: May increase bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulants such as warfarin.
Can interact with other medicines; medication review is recommended before use.
preparation: Most products use standardized leaf extracts; teas and non-standardized preparations are less comparable to trial products.
region: Ginkgo is native to China and now cultivated in multiple temperate regions.
therapeuticUses: Used for memory loss, dementia, and circulatory issues
summary: Ginkgo has been extensively studied, but NCCIH reports no conclusive evidence that it is helpful for any specific health condition.
